coredata.dc:title: Prominence of fusion temperature and engineering heteroatoms on multifarious emissive shifts in carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Velusamy, Jayaramakrishnan
Ramos-Ortiz, Gabriel
Rodríguez, Mario
Hernández-Cruz, Olivia
Ponce, Arturo
coredata.dc:description: We present a simple but robust strategy to engineering heteroatoms (N, S) on carbon dots (CDs) surface that results in a collection of enhanced photoluminescence (PL) emissions. The use of citric acid (CA) and 2-Imidazolidinethione (2-IZT) as precursors was envisioned to study the impact of thermolysis process on the PL properties. The proposed strategy, implemented at two different temperatures (180 °C and 200 °C), is suitable to produce CDs with tunable PL and quantum yield (QY) up to ∼32%. Similar to earlier reports of CA-based CDs, the self-assembling of fluorophores integrated into the CDs edge is hypothesized, however, in our CDs a double intriguing effect of blue- and red-shifting can be observed for PL as the wavelength of excitation is increased in the range 280–480 nm. Through a comprehensive characterization and evaluation of these CDs in water suspensions, the mechanisms that lead to PL multifarious emissions are proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Heteroatoms
Multifarious emission
Fluorophores
Citric acid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: G. Ramos-Ortiz acknowledges economical support from CONACyT ( Grant 215708 ). The microscopy work was supported by the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (NIMHD) in the program Research Centers in Minority Institutions Program (RCMI) Nanotechnology and Human Health Core (G12MD007591).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the one-pot synthetic strategy for simultaneously embedding N and S on CDs based on the use of 2-IZT as a sole heteroatoms source and CA as a carbon source. Temperatures of 180 °C and 200 °C are suitable to fetch the blue (b-CDs) and green (sg-CDs) emissive CDs, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Representative HRTEM images for the prepared b-CDs (upper panel) and sg-CDs (lower panel). The images at the center correspond to a single CD and depict the inter-planar spacing of the lattice, the inset displays the FFT of the CD where high frequency is showed. The size distribution histograms Gaussian fit is also presented.
Atomic weight composition (%) of C, N, S, and O for b-CDs and sg-CDs examined by EDX.

Normalized UV–vis absorption (blue line) and PL (black line) spectra for (a) b-CDs and (b) sg-CDs. PL spectra were obtained under excitation wavelengths at 338 nm and 350 nm for b-CDs and sg-CDs, respectively. The red line corresponds to excitation spectra with emission at 476 nm and 522 nm for b-CDs and sg-CDs, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Excitation-dependent emission spectra of b-CDs with maximum FWHM value of 108 nm at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Inset: the contour plot of b-CDs emission distribution.

(a) Intrinsic emissions from molecular fluorophores and extrinsic emissions from surface states of b-CDs exhibiting blue-shifts and red-shifts, respectively, as the excitation energy is increased. (b) PL lifetime detected at 478 nm (2.6 eV) for a water solution of b-CDs under the excitation at 405 nm (3.06 eV). The corresponding IRF curve is an internal reference for the equipment used to measure lifetimes.
Excitation-dependent emission spectra of sg-CDs with maximum FWHM value of 95 nm at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Inset: the contour plot of sg-CDs emission distribution.

(a) Intrinsic emissions from molecular fluorophores and extrinsic emissions from surface states of sg-CDs exhibiting blue-shifts and red-shifts, respectively, as the excitation energy is tuned. (b) PL lifetime detected at 523 nm (2.37 eV) for a water solution of sg-CDs under the excitation at 405 nm (3.06 eV). The corresponding IRF curve is an internal reference for the equipment used to measure lifetimes.
Proposed multifarious PL mechanisms for water solutions of b-CDs and sg-CDs.

head.ce:title: Prominence of fusion temperature and engineering heteroatoms on multifarious emissive shifts in carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigaciones en Óptica, A.P. 1-948, 37000 León, Gto, Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of Texas, San Antonio, TX 78249, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute for Astrophysics, Optics and Electronics (INAOE), Puebla 72000, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute for Astrophysics, Optics and Electronics (INAOE)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, low-cost and one-pot thermolysis strategy to fabricate N and S simultaneously embedded on carbon dots (CDs) from CA and 2-IZT. They demonstrated excellent multifarious photoluminescence emissive properties and can be promising zero-dimensional nanomaterials for further bioimaging and energy based applications.

We present a simple but robust strategy to engineering heteroatoms (N, S) on carbon dots (CDs) surface that results in a collection of enhanced photoluminescence (PL) emissions. The use of citric acid (CA) and 2-Imidazolidinethione (2-IZT) as precursors was envisioned to study the impact of thermolysis process on the PL properties. The proposed strategy, implemented at two different temperatures (180 °C and 200 °C), is suitable to produce CDs with tunable PL and quantum yield (QY) up to ∼32%. Similar to earlier reports of CA-based CDs, the self-assembling of fluorophores integrated into the CDs edge is hypothesized, however, in our CDs a double intriguing effect of blue- and red-shifting can be observed for PL as the wavelength of excitation is increased in the range 280–480 nm. Through a comprehensive characterization and evaluation of these CDs in water suspensions, the mechanisms that lead to PL multifarious emissions are proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive attention as candidates for next-generation of viable fluorescent probes and applications in bioimaging, photocatalysis, and optoelectronic devices [1]. CDs structure comprises sp2 hybridization or amorphous carbon, have typical sizes of less than 10 nm and exhibit attractive properties such as tunable photoluminescence (PL), biocompatibility, selectivity within the biological environment and high aqueous solubility at low-cost of fabrication [2]. However, the prevalent challenge for the exploitation of pristine CDs is their modest PL quantum yield (QY) [3,4]. Since the discovery of CDs, countless approaches, including surface passivation, functionalization, and dual heteroatoms (oxygen (O), nitrogen (N) or sulfur (S)) embedding were developed to increase their PL properties [5]. Compared to other approaches, by considering their merits, embedding heteroatoms on the CDs surface endure virtually all the advantages of pristine CDs and proficiently enhance PL [6]. For instance, Zhuo et al. [7] embedded N and S successfully on CDs by a facile method of synthesis. They prepared CDs comprising high QY (∼80.3%) with excitation-independent emission to be used as fluorescent biomarkers in the imaging of living A549 cells (adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells). Cui’s and Chen’s groups fabricated heteroatoms embedded CDs by hydrothermal method with effective fluorescent properties which were exploited for sensing Hg2+ in biological environments [8,9]. Many other studies have also realized the importance of implanting heteroatoms (N, S) for tweaking the optical properties of CDs as a condition for their use in diverse fields [10–15]. However, there are still scientific aspects to be addressed for the better understanding of the PL properties of CDs embedded with heteroatoms, i.e., the mechanisms that produce multifarious PL features and excitation-dependent fluorescence.

Some groups have reported CDs with PL spectrum comprising multiple colors, suggesting different mechanisms as the origin of such feature: arrangement of electron-hole pairs, quantum effects, surface functional groups, surface states, molecular states, carbon core and surface domains of fluorophores with different degrees of conjugation [16–20]. In CDs, the PL is directly related to the energy gaps between two energy levels. Although it is well known that the use of O, N, S, and P (phosphorus) or related chemical groups can tailor band gaps when such elements are incorporated into the external lattice framework of CDs [3,8,13–15,21], the mechanisms that lead to multicolor PL emission are not always known.

In addition to the mechanism mentioned above for tailoring the band structure by embedding heteroatoms, in the past few years, small molecules (fluorophores) have been integrated into CDs [22–26]. Especially, CDs prepared from citric acid (CA) in combination with amino group-containing molecules exhibited higher QY (>80%) compared with those synthesized with other precursors [22–34]. It is hypothesized that the PL emission originates not only from the sp2/sp3 carbon structure and surface passivation groups but also possibly from the presence of fluorophores implanted on the CDs edge (forming edge states or molecular states). For instance, during the fabrication of CDs, a variety of fluorophores can be self-synthesized producing edge states. Intermolecular or covalent forces usually bind these fluorescent sites and π-conjugated domains enhance their integration in the carbon core. The π-conjugated domains can in turn favor charge transfer process in the CDs [30,34]. However, in most of the reported works the band gap structure is tuned based on N-containing groups. Therefore, developing a strategy for the integration of luminescent molecules and optimizing emissive sites in CA-based CDs through charge transfer effects along with passivated dual heteroatoms (N, S) is still an open challenge. Such strategy might pave the way for understanding the combined impact of various heteroatoms substituents linked with luminescent molecules. Also, the multifarious PL on these CDs will extend the frontier of their practical consumption in a multiplicity of areas such as bioimaging, displaying, lighting and so forth.

Taking the above considerations into account, herein we report a robust but straightforward one-pot synthesis based in thermolysis to generate CDs with multifarious emissive properties and high QY up to ∼32 %. The synthesis approach is based on the use of the precursors CA and the molecule 2-Imidazolidinethione (2-IZT). We envisioned that 2-IZT, which has not been used before in CDs synthesis, is an advantageous precursor material since it contains both N and S elements for the sole doping heteroatoms source; further, it also produces self-assembled luminescent molecules on the CDs edge. In this strategy, a first optical tuning of PL characteristics is achieved by simply tuning the 2-IZT mass proportion and temperature of the thermolysis process. After discussing the optimization process of synthesis parameters to fabricate CDs at two different temperatures, the morphology, structure and chemical composition are carefully characterized. We then focus on the optical properties of such CDs systematically studied in water suspension. With an emphasis on excitation-dependence effects, we discuss the influence of the fluorophores that hypothetically formed during the synthesis as well as surface heteroatoms on the PL properties of CDs. Finally, a reasonable mechanism is proposed to explain the multifarious PL behaviors of the prepared CDs. The outcome of these results may provide insights into the PL mechanism of the CDs with edge state molecules linked with heteroatoms.

Starting materials (CA and 2-IZT) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Mexico) and used as were received. In a typical preparation procedure, CA (101 mg) and 2-IZT (161 mg) were dissolved in ultrapure Millipore water and then sonicated to make the solute completely dispersed. With the use of an Ace pressure tube flask from Sigma-Aldrich, the suspension was kept at 70 °C in a magnetic stirrer during 12 h (h) for vaporization of the solvent. Additionally, 10 ml of ultrapure water was added to the resulted dense syrup, and then the solution was stirred and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After the reaction was completed, the flask was cooled to room temperature. The final product appearance was a semi-transparent solution with blue emission under the excitation of a hand-held ultraviolet (UV) lamp. In the second set of samples the same procedure was repeated but in this case at 200 °C; in this case, green emission was obtained from dark yellow-brown like a solution.

Aqueous solutions of CDs were ultra-centrifuged step-by-step by means of different centrifugation swiftness from 3000 to 14500 rpm generating different accelerations up to 16,000 g for 20 min. This process removed the non-fluorescent deposits in each centrifuge and collected the upper aqueous solution. Then a filtering process through a PTFE membrane with pore size of 0.20 µm (Thermofisher Scientific) removed large particles. Subsequently, CDs were collected by dialysis against ultrapure water: solutions of CDs were loaded in dialysis bags (SpectraPor with molecular weight cut-off of MWCO: 500–1000 D) for 2 days (water changed every 12 h). The prepared CDs were stored in the dark at 4 °C to produce a solid through lyophilization. CDs were dispersed in water at a concentration of 0.45 mg/mL. On the whole of this report, the CDs synthesized at 180 °C and 200 °C, are denoted as b-CDs and sg-CDs, respectively, due to their characteristic blue (b) and relatively strong green (sg) fluorescence intensity for the bare eye visualization (see photograph in Supplementary Information, Fig. S1). Further detailed information on apparatus and techniques employed for the full characterization of the obtained CDs is given Supplementary Information.

Fig. 1 shows a scheme of the one-pot synthetic strategy (thermolysis in water) for simultaneously embedding N and S on CDs. Several factors, including the concentration of N and S source in combination with CA and the temperature of the reaction, can affect the photoluminescence of N, S embedded CDs. Therefore, optimization of experimental conditions (mass and temperature) is essential to achieve N, S embedded CDs with excellent fluorescence properties. Initially, the mass ratio of 2-IZT was adjusted from 0.5 to 4, maintaining constant other parameters such as CA mass and temperature (180 °C) for the time of 24 h. The prepared samples were observed under illumination from a UV lamp (∼365 nm) to corroborate whether they merited for progressive investigation (Fig. S2). The mass ratio of 1:3 (CA:2-IZT) was the optimal fraction for generating the strongest PL emissions. The lower or higher N and S source of mass ratios produced CDs with reduced PL intensity. The reduced PL at higher proportion could be possibly due to the blockage of the passivated surface defects by the excess of N, S in the ensemble. The effect of reaction temperature on the fluorescence of as-synthesized CDs for the optimal mass ratio of 1:3(CA:2-IZT) was also investigated with the following temperatures 180 °C, 200 °C, 220 °C, 250 °C and 280 °C. The carbonization temperature and final synthesis procedure were selected by considering the range for thermal decomposition path of CA (>160 °C to <220 °C) [35,36] and also the obtained optical (PL emission) properties of the resulted CDs data (Fig. S3). The crucial temperatures (for fusion reaction) that produced CDs with distinctive PL properties were determined as 180 and 200 °C. For practical applications, the PL stability performance is very important. In this work, the PL stability performance was scrutinized under the illumination of UV irradiation for a period of time 6 h and the results are depicted in Fig. S4. Both b-CDs and sg-CDs were exhibited an excellent photo stability performance (∼95 %) over 6 h of UV illumination exposure of 18 mW/cm2 (from 125 W Hg lamp). Hence, the temperature interval proposed in this work could produce distinct and stable optical properties for practical applications.

Generally, during the thermolysis reaction, CA’s low carbonization temperature (≤200 °C) not only used as a carbon source to easily assemble the nano-template structure but also facilitates the surface attaching of O and N elements through intermolecular hydrogen bonds (H-bonds). Often, molecules containing N and S, specifically those with short chains, are used as effective passivating candidates for CDs [7,12,14]. We envisioned that the molecular structure of 2-IZT was a suitable sole doping source to provide the heteroatoms N and S on the surface of CD through a facile thermolysis route simultaneously. We also hypothesized that under hydrothermal conditions, CA and 2-IZT could produce self-assembled fluorophores on the edge of CDs by condensation or polymerization process [27,32,37]. Due to the hydrothermal treating of mixtures of CA with the amide-based molecular precursors (2-IZT) favors ring-closing reactions and forms citrazinic acid or pyridine/imidazole ring-derivatives with H-bonding [30,33,38,39]. These pyridine-derivatives/citrazinic acid might act as independent fluorescent sites, or possibly might be linked to the surface or incorporated into the carbon core [27,29,31,40].

CD surface groups, i.e., pyridine derivatives, are in direct contact with the solvent and can form H-bonding interactions [33,41,42]. Hence surface groups are susceptible to establish a network through intermolecular H bonds. Because the amide (NH) group is soluble in water, amino (NH2) groups can be produced by intermolecular H-bonds. This intermolecular H-bonding leads to blue PL in b-CDs. However, when the fusion temperature is increased from 180 °C to 200 °C, a different configuration of pyridine-derivatives on the edge state of sg-CDs could take place. Moreover, the higher reaction temperature could lead to the formation of CDs with higher S content and consequently a longer wavelength of photoluminescent emissions [10]. In this condition of thermolysis of higher temperature, the intermolecular H-bonds between NH groups and water molecules are weakened [33], resulting in the slow burning of the surface-bounded N (amide) and the creation of intramolecular H-bonds. Generally, the intermolecular H-bonds are more sensitive to temperature and pressure than the intramolecular H-bonds [42,43]. In the case of sg-CDs, the increment of surface passivation with S groups along with intramolecular H-bonds conversion could be the reason for brighter and longer wavelength (green) emission compared with b-CDs. Further, the increment of temperature in the thermolysis process could play a role not only in steering the surface passivation with heteroatoms but also in generating intramolecular H-bonds with fluorophores (with different structures) and surface groups. The influence of the thermolysis temperature in the one-pot syntheses and a mechanism of formation of CDs are hypothesized in Fig. S5. Further discussion is provided after presenting the optical properties of the CDs.

The size distribution and morphology of CDs were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) as shown in Fig. 2 . The histograms in this figure were calculated from a population of 100 CDs located at random locations on the grid by using the ImageJ software (National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD, USA). The results manifest that the prepared S and N embedded CDs has similar size distributions in the range 2.0–6.0 nm. Fitting the histogram to a Gaussian model yielded a mean diameter of 3.5 nm, with a standard deviation of 0.4 nm, which emphasis the size monodisperse nature of the CDs and these values are in good agreement with other N and S embedded CDs [15,21,44]. Moreover, it was observed from HRTEM images of single CD particle that the inter-planar spacing of the b-CDs and sg-CDs are 0.231 nm and 0.238 nm, respectively. This is reliable to the (0 0 2) face of amorphous carbon which is composed of aromatic carbon oriented in a significantly random manner [45]. According to X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) results (Fig.S6, Supporting Information), both b-CDs and sg-CDs reveal a broad diffraction peak centered around 2θ ∼ 23.28° and 23.68°, which was attributed to highly disordered carbon phase [46,47]. Notice that the interlayer spacing values are higher than in graphite (3.34 Å), probably due to the introduction of N, S, and O-containing functional groups [15,20,47].

Raman spectra of b-CDs and sg-CDs are shown in Fig. S7. Two dominant peaks centered around 1380 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 are identified as D and G bands, respectively. D band arises from vibrations of sp3 -hybridized carbon atoms with hanging bonds or disordered carbon atoms [48]. The G band is associated with the E2g mode of graphite due to the in-plane bond stretching vibration motion of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice [49,50]. The ratio between the areas under the curves corresponding to the D and G peaks (ID/IG) is often used to assess the structural purity, e.g., whether the nature of the nanostructured carbon material is amorphous or crystalline [15,51]. The values of ID/IG in b-CDs (1.94) and sg-CDs (2.01) imply the presence of high-density defects (or higher disorder) and high-concentration of dopants on the defect sites of the CDs, i.e., the Raman spectra indicates that the fabricated CDs consists mainly an amorphous carbon nanostructure.

Fig. S8 presents the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of b-CDs and sg-CDs. The strong broad bands from 3600 to 3100 cm−1 corresponding to υ(NH)/υ(OH) should be generally attributed to the presence of amide groups (NH secondary) and alcohol (OH) of fluorophores; the latter also suggests a highly hydrophilic nature of the prepared CDs. The presence of the peak between 1640 and 1690 cm−1 are assigned to CO, CN, and CC vibrations and reveal that the combination of carbonyl and carboxyl functionalities of sp2 carbon. The regions around 1395–1570 cm−1, 1260–1420 cm−1, 940–1140 cm−1 are due to the mixed vibrations of NCS/CS of fluorophores. Weak vibrations were detected around 2300 cm−1 due to SH and CN. In addition, the vibrational frequencies around ∼1370 cm−1 and 800 cm−1 correspond to CH bond of sp3 and sp2 aromatic carbon, respectively. Therefore, FTIR analysis reveals that in both CDs, N and S groups are linked to sp2 and sp3 carbons.

The chemical composition analysis of b-CDs and sg-CDs were probed by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Fig. S9). The peaks in EDX spectra of b-CDs and sg-CDs are assigned to C, O, N, and S; their corresponding atomic weight ratio values are presented in Fig. 3 . The N/S atomic mass ratio values from EDX measurements are 4.73:1 and 1.18:1 for the b-CDs and sg-CDs, respectively. b-CDs have richer N-contents in their surface state while sg-CDs exhibits a reduction of N-contents and about 3 fold increase of S contents. This fact suggests that during thermolysis at the temperature at which sg-CDs were fabricated, the amino groups (NH2) were not stable and diminished their concentration at the surface [42]. It has been reported that N and S dual embedded CDs usually exhibit good catalytic activity to eliminate O compared with the N-rich CDs [6]. The atomic mass in sg-CDs (1.1%) is much lower (5 fold) than in b-CDs, which means that significant portion of O was eliminated during the thermolysis at a temperature of 200 °C.

UV–visible absorption experiments were conducted in water suspensions (see Fig. 4 ). The absorption bands at 245 nm (5.06 eV) and 238 nm (5.21 eV) correspond to the electronic transition S0 → S2 for b-CDs and sg-CDs, respectively. They are typically attributed to π-π* transitions within the sp2- localized carbon core of CDs [6,52–57]. Generally, CDs comprise π-conjugated domains which are either subunit of graphene or amorphous carbon composed of inhomogeneous sp2 with an energy gap in the range from 1.8 to 7 eV [38]. Hence, both CDs show large band gap values (>5 eV) associated with nano sp2-localized carbon core embedded in sp3 hybridized carbon matrix. The well-pronounced low energy bands (S0 → S1) centered at 338 nm (3.66 eV) and 350 nm (3.54 eV) along with their extended absorption tails (∼500 nm) feature n–π* edge transitions [27,33,58,59] in b-CDs and sg-CDs, respectively. At this point, the edge states stand for molecular fluorophore, as they form the boundary between sp2 - and sp3 - hybridized carbon (edge of CD core); they are the main emissive centers for the bright luminescence in CDs involving both n → π* (edge) and π → π*(charge transfer) transitions. These transitions are due to the creation of new surface traps by surface passivation groups as well as charge transfer between the contribution of small fluorophore molecules and CDs core, respectively [27,33,58,59]. Some research groups have suggested that the absorption peak around 320–350 nm, generally observed in the CA-based CDs, is due to the presence of pyridine-derivative molecules [24,25,27–30]. Notice that the low-lying energy band (S0 → S1) shown in Fig. 4 is in agreement with the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of pyridine-derivatives [27]. Moreover, the long absorption tail extended beyond 410 nm is characteristic of luminescent pyridine-derivatives integrated into the CDs core. These tails are due to the creation of new surface traps by surface passivation groups as well as the contribution of small fluorophore molecules in the CDs [27,33,58,59]. The observed red-shift (∼12 nm) emission of sg-CDs compared b-CDs is a consequence of different molecular fluorophore formation and their linking with surface groups during the condensation process at 200 °C. Thus, by just tuning the fusion temperature from our one-pot thermolysis strategy, sg-CDs and b-CDs CDs have distinctive surface states and generate different electronic energy band gaps.

To further explore the electronic transitions, the contribution of carbon core, the presence of luminescent molecules and the influence of surface passivation in both CDs, a series of PL spectroscopy measurements were conducted. First, we discuss the PL properties of b-CDs for excitation wavelengths from 280 nm (4.42 eV) to 480 nm (2.58 eV) in steps of 10 nm (Fig. 5 ). For excitation wavelengths in the range from of 280 nm to 340 nm, the PL spectra of b-CDs are characterized by multifarious emissive peaks at visible wavelengths which undergo increments in their intensities and continuous blue-shifts from 523 nm to 476 nm. In contrast, when the excitation wavelength is tuned above 340 nm, the emissions at visible wavelengths display a continuous red-shifting from 478 nm (2.59 eV) to 542 nm (2.28 eV) and decrease in intensity. Unlike many other reported CDs containing citrazinic acid-based or pyridine-based fluorophores as well as embedded N and S heteroatoms, the CDs here reported exhibit full-color emission with a double intriguing effect of blue-shifts and red-shifts. Such effect has not been observed in previous reports on CDs [21,59–61]. The excitation wavelength at 340 nm exhibits the maximum full width at half maximum (FWHM) value of 108 nm, which elucidates that less homogeneity in size distribution of the b-CDs nanoparticles [36]. The spectra exhibit weak PL emissions at UV wavelengths region (300–350 nm) is due to the water Raman PL peaks.

The overall excitation energy dependence of PL emission from b-CDs at visible wavelengths (blue-shifts and red-shifts) and the corresponding intensities are depicted in Fig. 6 a. In this figure, the maximum PL intensity is achieved for excitation at 340 nm (PL emission at 478 nm). At these wavelengths of excitation and emission, the QY of b-CDs was determined by using the integrating sphere (IS) method and a comparative method (Fig. S8). The results from both approaches were in good agreement with a QY ∼ 25.1%. The PL decay curve for b-CDs obtained at photon emission energy of 2.6 eV (405 nm) and excitation energy of 3.06 eV (405 nm) (this energy corresponds to the laser line available in our fluorometer to measure lifetimes) is presented in Fig. 6b. The PL decay curve is well fitted by a bi-exponential function. The two distinct relaxation time constants, τ1 (fast decay) and τ2 (slow decay) and proportions of their intensities were used to determine an average lifetime (τ ave) of fluorescence, which was calculated from the Eq. (1) given Supplementary Information. The calculated τ ave of b-CDs is 4.19 ns.

As for sg-CDs, the PL spectra also have blue-shifted multifarious emissive states corresponding to excitations from 280 nm (4.42 eV) to 340 nm (3.64 eV) as shown in Fig. 7 , while exciting between 350 nm (3.64 eV) and 460 nm (2.69 eV) lead to an abrupt PL red-shifting over 70 nm, residing between 517 nm and 595 nm. The inset of Fig. 7 shows PL contour plot of sg-CDs, which is relatively better homogeneous PL emissive distribution than b-CDs. The excitation wavelength at 350 nm exhibits the maximum full width at half maximum (FWHM) value of 95 nm. The FWHM below 100 nm acknowledges a homogeneity in size distribution of the sg-CDs [36]. The maximum QY (∼32.5%) of the sg-CDs was obtained for the excitation wavelength of 350 nm corresponding to emission of 523 nm. The overall excitation energy dependence of the PL emissions (blue-shifts and red-shifts) and the corresponding intensities from the sg-CDs are depicted in Fig. 8 a. PL lifetime decay is presented in Fig. 8b. This curve was fitted by a bi-exponential function resulting in a τ ave ∼ 4.71 ns at the PL maximum peak wavelength and corresponding is depicted in Fig. 8b.

Based on the multifarious PL emissions and spectral shifts exhibited by the fabricated CDs, herein we propose the PL mechanisms depicted schematically in Fig. 9 . This figure first illustrates different emissive colors (blue and green) based on the fusion temperature (180 °C or 200 °C) used during the synthesis process. The figure also illustrates the intriguing PL peak shifts: (i) blue-shifts for excitation in the range 280–340 nm; (ii) red-shifts for excitation in the range 340–480 nm. Some questions about the PL behavior of CDs need to be addressed. First of all, how do PL studies could probe that luminescent pyridine-derivatives exist in the edges of both CDs? Secondly, why do b-CDs and sg-CDs show different PL emission colors and QYs?

We assume the formation of pyridine-derivative luminescent molecules integrated to CDs core by carboxylic junctions. This possibility is a well-established concept in CA-based CDs reported by various research groups [27,29,30]. Generally, carboxylic junctions can serve as fast pathways of charge-transfer from carbon core to luminescent molecules [34]. Hence, the pyridine-derivatives could play more important role on the main intense PL emissions of CDs. While exciting the CDs optically, excited sp2 core carbons transfer their charges to luminescent molecules creating edge/molecular states or intrinsic state emissions. The antibonding state of pyridine-derivative (π*) could be responsible for receiving charges through carboxylic junctions. As a consequence, they increase the electron clouds in the edge state, which lift the Fermi energy levels then emitting different energies. In this case, intrinsic states of CDs exhibit blue-shifted emissions until the excitation wavelength of 340 nm is reached (Figs. 6a and 8a). In fact, in b-CDs and sg-CDs the PL maximum intensity is observed at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Notice that some reports show the maximum emission intensities of pyridine-derivatives [29,30] precisely at 340 nm of excitation. This suggests that the formation of molecular/edge states on the CDs based on different luminescent molecular structures (pyridine derivatives) might be responsible for generating intrinsic blue-shift emissions until 340 nm.

Interestingly, red-shifting of PL emissions started from 350 nm of excitation, which may be due to the contribution of surface traps or defect state groups (N, S) in both CDs. This typical red-shift phenomenon is associated with extrinsic states and can be induced by cooperative effects of N and S on the surface [6,12,62]. In this manner, the increment of electron density (cloud) by the presence of lone pair electrons in N and S functional groups reduces the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO for the extrinsic emissive state. S and N are preferably incorporated into the neighboring C and H-bonds with a strong affinity (water suspension). The FTIR spectra were presented in Fig. S10 shows that both CDs contain NH, OH, and CO groups, ascribed to the fact that groups derived CA play a major role in the surface state formation. Hence, surface groups on the CDs could be formed by intramolecular H-bonds, which may create complex states (traps) in CDs. These intramolecular H-bonds could be destroyed by the surrounding polar water molecules (used as a solvent in this work) to form intermolecular H-bonds between the CA groups and H2O for b-CDs prepared at 180 °C. Hence, in our proposed one-pot strategy, the blue color emission is attributed to a surface state formed by intermolecular H-bonds in b-CDs. In sg-CDs, green emission was associated with the surface state formed by intramolecular H-bonds when increasing reaction temperature to 200 °C. As we discussed earlier, due to the increment of the fusion temperature from 180 °C to 200 °C, intermolecular H-bonds between the CA groups and molecules (especially NH2) are greatly weakened. The resulted sg-CDs were formed by the surface intramolecular H-bonds to create surface groups (see Fig S5).

b-CDs are characterized by a QY of 24.9 % (SI method) which is smaller than the value of 32.1 % obtained for sg-CDs. This is due to the stronger electronegativity of N (χ = 3.04 on the Pauling scale) compared to S (χ = 2.51), which leads to photo-excited electrons loss during internal photon conversion in the b-CDs [42,58]. The increment of QY value as well as maximum emission intensity in sg-CDs, can also be influenced by the different type of pyridine derivative structural configuration. The difference of QY is in agreement with the PL decay time, e.g., the sg-CDs have a longer average lifetime (4.71 ns) than b-CDs (4.19 ns). PL decay pathways indicate that the surface traps emissions of the CDs are not an independent process. Instead, it should be affected by the combination of molecular states and defect traps, leading to the decrease in PL lifetime. In brief, the optical properties of the CDs result from a combination of effects from pyridine-derivative molecules and surface state traps.

In summary, we successfully fabricated dual heteroatoms (N, S) embedded CDs with multifarious optical properties (alternating blue-shift and red-shift are clearly observed in PL) via one-pot thermolysis of simple precursors (CA and 2-IZT) for the first time. The implementation of two optimized temperatures (180 °C and 200 °C) and systematic characterization methods have conclusively supported the formation of CDs composed mainly of the amorphous structure along with different self-assembled pyridine-derivatives on the edge of CDs. Bestowing to the constructed PL mechanism model it follows that (i) there exits pyridine-derivatives covalently connected to the CDs edge, acting as the primary luminescent centers to produce intrinsic PL emissions which undergo blue-shifting as the wavelength excitation wavelength increases until 340 nm; they receive charges through carboxylic bonds connected on CDs edge; (ii) the different emission colors from the CDs are mainly depended on the PL properties of self-assembled pyridine-derivatives structure and the establishing different H-bonding with surface groups. The QY of the CDs depends mostly on the cooperative effect of the pyridine-derivatives molecule and number of surface traps (N and S). This implies that CDs of different PL colors can be fabricated by just tuning the reaction temperature. The favorable photophysical properties and excitation-dependent behavior of the CDs will provide a way to optimize the emission wavelength and offer new insight into rational design of efficient supermolecular hybrids based on fluorescent CDs for protein delivery systems, and may open up new opportunities for both fundamental and applied research of CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: G. Ramos-Ortiz acknowledges economical support from CONACyT (Grant 215708). The microscopy work was supported by the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (NIMHD) in the program Research Centers in Minority Institutions Program (RCMI) Nanotechnology and Human Health Core (G12MD007591).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.05.040.

ce:section.ce:para:
